title: SEGMENT 14 — The FDA pathway: IND, compassionate use, and the road to approval

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The FDA pathway: IND, compassionate use, and the road to approval
How does any of this actually get authorized?
Because you don't just decide to put a pig kidney in a person.
You go through the FDA.
The xenotransplantation pathway at the FDA is run by a part of the agency called the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research, or CBER.
CBER is the part of the FDA that regulates products that derive from living systems — vaccines, gene therapies, cell therapies, and yes, organs from genetically engineered animals.
Each pig organ is, in CBER's framework, a kind of biological product, subject to the same broad regulatory architecture as a gene therapy or a vaccine.
The principal regulatory document is called an Investigational New Drug application, or IND.

To run a clinical trial with a xenograft, the sponsor — usually the company, sometimes the academic medical center — submits an IND to CBER describing the donor pig, the genetic edits, the manufacturing process, the surgical protocol, the immunosuppression, the surveillance plan, the patient selection criteria, and the proposed clinical endpoints.
CBER reviews the IND, asks questions, sometimes requires additional preclinical data, and, if satisfied, allows the trial to proceed.
The cases we've talked about so far — Bennett, Faucette, Slayman, Pisano, Looney — were not done under formal INDs in the trial sense.

They were done under a special pathway called expanded access, sometimes called compassionate use, which allows the FDA to authorize the use of an experimental therapy in a single patient who has no other options.
Each case required its own separate FDA authorization.
The team had to make the case, in writing, that this particular patient had no alternative, that the risks were understood, that the patient had consented.
The agency had to agree, case by case.
The next step — the one the field is actively working on — is moving from individual expanded-access cases to a formal Phase 1 clinical trial.

A Phase 1 xeno trial would enroll a defined cohort of patients, probably starting with six to ten, with pre-specified inclusion criteria, a uniform surgical protocol, a defined immunosuppression regimen, and predefined endpoints.
The first such trials are expected to begin in 2025, with eGenesis and United Therapeutics — the parent of Revivicor — each pursuing their own programs.
If those trials are successful, the next phase would be Phase 2 and Phase 3 trials, with larger patient cohorts and randomization to compare xeno to standard of care.

The full pathway to FDA approval — to the point where a doctor can prescribe a pig kidney without a special authorization — is generally estimated, by people in the field, to require first approvals around 2027 to 2028.
That timeline could slip — it almost certainly will, in some direction — but the order of magnitude is right.
Within five years, we may have an approved xenograft.
Within ten, it could be a standard of care for the kidney waiting list.
One more wrinkle worth mentioning.

The FDA, in 2003, published a major guidance document for xenotransplantation that laid out, in detail, the source-animal requirements, the screening requirements, the patient surveillance requirements, and the reporting requirements.
That document has been updated periodically since, most recently in light of the Bennett experience.

Among other things, the guidance requires that xenotransplant patients agree to lifelong surveillance — periodic blood testing for pig pathogens — and that they accept restrictions on certain activities like blood donation.
Some critics have argued that the surveillance requirements amount to a permanent restriction on personal autonomy.
The counterargument is that the public health stakes — preventing a novel zoonotic disease — justify the constraint.
The debate continues.
